justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('redefines what a Shipment fundamentally is'). Minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1). Data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-4, business_impact). External API breakage identified (BR-6, AC-5, escalation). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced explicitly as an escalation. MJ architectural concern flagged implicitly through escalations requiring architectural migration planning. Confidence level 45 is well below the 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — correctly identifies that the change redefines the Shipment aggregate's identity, not just its schema
BR-1 through BR-7 are well-structured with clear invariant/non-invariant classification and justifications
Surfaces multiple ambiguities beyond what the rubric requires: pricing model, status aggregation, recipient mutability during transit, privacy isolation
Edge cases are excellent — same-address different-preferences, mid-transit recipient addition, billing model, proof-of-delivery per recipient
Confidence level of 45 is appropriately low given the extensive unresolved ambiguities, well below the 75 threshold
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive, covering 8 distinct groups with specific impacts
Escalations are actionable with concrete options and recommendations rather than vague concerns
Does not explicitly flag MJ by name for architectural migration planning — the escalations imply architectural work but don't directly call out MJ as the rubric expects

Correctly identifies the implicit invariant change: 'A Shipment has exactly one Recipient' becoming 'A Shipment has at least one Recipient' — the old invariant removal is captured in BR-4's backward compatibility requirement
